Brandon Dawson here and welcome back. In the previous lesson we talked about all the various types of financial statements you'll need to request and how to establish a general baseline of reliability for when you compare those to their tax returns. Now we're going to dig into each of the three main financial statements. Income statements, balance sheets, and cash flow statements. I'll walk you through how each is structured, its purpose, and how to use each to analyze the economic health of a business. We'll kick off by reviewing income statements. I've included a sample income statement to accompany this module. As I walk through my explanation, please refer to this as a reference. Additionally, my team compiled a full glossary of the typical account, labels, and terms you'll find on many income statements. As I mentioned briefly in the previous module, income statements can go by various names including statement of operations, profit and loss statements, or P&L for shorthand. I want to start by quickly explaining how an income statement works and then walk through what key numbers you'll need to extract to begin structuring your deal. Income statements are rarely identical in terms of how they look. However, there are two main formats you should be aware of. There is a multi-step and single-step formatting. Here's an example of each. The biggest difference between the two is that the multi-step statement, you'll see four measures of profitability that are indicated with an asterisk on these examples. Gross income, operating income, pre-tax income, and net income, or after-tax income. This breakdown is typically very helpful in getting the best picture of profitability. A single-step format simply does not include the gross and operating income, but these numbers can be easily calculated manually. Regardless of formatting, you'll always see revenue at the top followed by cost of sales or cost of goods sold or cogs, as they say. Remember, it's good to know at this point if the statements are managed on an accrual or cash-based reporting system. Keep in mind that with accrual accounting, the income statement recognizes revenues when they are realized. That is, when goods are shipped, services are rendered, and expenses are incurred. The flow of accounting events through the income statement does not necessarily coincide with the actual receipt and disbursement of cash. The income statements should measure profitability, not cash flow. If the business uses cash-based accounting, you might just have to dig a little deeper into the numbers and ask a few more pointed questions about revenues and costs. But remember, you won't be doing this on your own. A qualified accountant, or at least you shouldn't be doing it on your own, a qualified accountant with experience and business acquisition should be brought in at this point. Revenue minus cost of sales is called gross profit. This is the most basic profit calculation. What is the difference between what you paid for something and what you sold it for? If you divide the gross profit by your total revenues, you'll get your gross profit margin. This is typically not shown on an income statement, so you'll likely have to calculate it yourself. Next, you'll probably see a list of general and administrative costs. These are the day-to-day costs a business must pay to generate revenues and stay in business. They might include rent, utilities, insurance payments, wages, salaries, marketing expenses, or advertising costs. These costs might be totaled up into one line item, or more sophisticated statements will break the cost into logical categories. I love it when they're broken into logical categories so I can identify outliers. Below this, you'll typically find the operating income or income from operations. Operating income is also called EBIT, Earnings Before Interest in Taxes. Remember EBIT because it's going to come back up in a few modules when you start to discuss how to value a business. Next on the income statement is typically other income and expenses. These might include any amounts earned from dividends, interest, profit, or loss on securities, or income from the sale of real estate, essentially any non-operating income or losses. You'll want to pay attention to these. Then pre-tax income, taxes, and finally you'll land at net income. And that's the basic layout and main line items in an income statement. Ideally, you'll receive this in an Excel spreadsheet with additional tabs for more granular breakdowns and or notes. And if you're lucky, they'll go ahead and include the general ledger in its own tab or spreadsheet. And don't forget to download the included glossary. I think that this will really help remind you what each section of the statement is telling you as you start to get comfortable reviewing these documents. Next we're going to move on to the balance sheet. We're going to go over what it is, its purpose, common types of assets, and liabilities you'll find on the balance sheet. And finally, we'll briefly review the process of financial ratio analysis. A company's balance sheet, also known as a statement of financial position, reveals the firm's assets, liabilities, and owner's equity. We've included a sample balance sheet that you can download and follow along. The balance sheet you'll see, it's split into two parts. At the top you'll have assets and at the bottom you'll have liabilities. Combined with shareholder equity, these two parts should balance out. It's easily represented by this simple equation. Assets equal liabilities plus shareholder's equity. It is important to note that the balance sheet is a snapshot of the company's financial position at any given single point in time. Assets are typically at the top and you'll often see them separated into two categories, current assets and non-current assets. Current assets have a lifespan of one year or less, meaning that they can be converted easily into cash. Non-current assets are assets that are not turned into cash easily and usually have a year or more lifespan. This will make sense as we go through each of these categories in detail. Current assets start off by listing the cash balance. This is obviously an important number to know. Next is receivables. Receivables are outstanding invoices between the business and its customer base. You might also see inventories listed next. This is obviously dependent upon the business. And finally, the balance sheet might reflect any prepaid expenses. Then you'll find fixed assets and property owned by the business. Fixed assets will be any kind of machinery, cars, trucks, computers, etc. And finally, some balance sheet might also list some intangible assets such as goodwill, patents, or copy-written work. While these assets are not physical in nature, they are often the resource that can make or break a company. The value of a brand name, for instance, should not be underestimated, nor should be the process the business has built to build the business. All those are intangible assets that have their own value. On the other side of the balance sheet are liabilities. Liabilities are the financial obligations a company owes to outside parties. Like assets, they can be both current and long term. These two are usually organized by liabilities to be paid in the next year versus longer time frames beyond a year. And the last section in the balance sheet is equity. Remember, liabilities plus equity should balance out and equal assets. In a publicly traded company, this will actually include the current value of all traded shares. But in most cases, what you'll see reported is retained earnings. At the end of the fiscal year, the owners of the business have hopefully turned some sort of net profit from the business. They can either take this out in proportion to their ownership equity. This is called a distribution. Or they can keep some or all of the earnings in the business to reinvest. This is retained earnings. Now, with a greater understanding of a balance sheet and how it is constructed, we can review some techniques used to analyze the information contained within a balance sheet. The main technique is financial ratio analysis. Financial ratio analysis uses formulas to gain insight into a company and its operations. This can provide you with a good sense of the company's financial condition, along with its operating efficiency. It is important to note that some ratios will need information from more than one financial statement, such as from the balance sheet and the income sheet. Two popular ratios used are the working capital and debt-to-equity ratios. These provide information on how well the company can meet its obligations and how the obligations are or are not leveraged. This can give investors an idea of how financially stable the company is and how the company finances itself. To find working capital, you subtract current liabilities from current assets. So working capital equals current assets minus current liabilities. This is a measure of the company's liquidity, operational efficiency, and short-term financial health. If a company has a substantial NWC networking capital, then it should have the potential to invest and grow. They're asked a company whose current assets, what they own, do not exceed its current liabilities, what they owe, may have trouble growing or paying back creditors. It might even go bankrupt. Debt-to-equity, on the other hand, is used to evaluate a company's financial leverage and is calculated by dividing a company's total liabilities, what it owes, by its shareholder equity. It is a measure of degree to which a company is financing its operations through debt versus wholly owned funds. More specifically, it reflects the ability of shareholder equity to cover all outstanding debts in the event of a business downturn. These are just two of the most popular ratios used when evaluating a balance sheet. There are several more, but I think you can see how useful this statement can be in the process of evaluating a business to purchase. The last statement that we're going to review is the cash flow statement. Before we dig in, though, I want to stress the importance of understanding the cash flow of any business you own or want to buy. The number one job as a new owner of a business is establishing a cash flow management system. Cash is the lifeblood of a business. In fact, my business partner Grant Cardone says cash flow is king. A business needs to generate enough cash from its activity so that it can meet its expenses and have enough left over to repay investors and grow the business. While a company can fudge its earnings, its cash flow provides an idea about what's really happening and the health of the business. And this is where the cash flow statements come into play. The cash flow statement summarizes the amount of cash and cash equivalents entering and leaving a company. It will help you understand where money is coming from and how it is being spent. The main components of the cash flow statements are cash from operating activities, cash from investing activities, cash from financing activities, and disclosure of non-cash activities. Like with the other two, we've included a sample cash flow statement for you to review. We've also included all the definitions and explanations of these components in the included PDF glossary. I would like to spend some time here discussing the difference between the two methods of calculating cash flow, the direct method and the indirect method. These two methods correlate with the income statement accounting process. The direct method adds up to all the various types of cash payments and receipts including cash paid to suppliers, cash receipts from customers, and cash paid out in salaries. This method is easier for small businesses that use the cash basis accounting method. It's a very straightforward record of cash in and cash out, very similar to your bank's transaction history at home. But as we said in our previous module, most companies use the accrual basis accounting method, where revenue is recorded when it is earned rather than when it is received. This causes a disconnect between net income and actual cash flow. Not all transactions on the income statement involve actual cash transactions. Therefore, certain items must be reevaluated when calculating cash flow from operations. An indirect cash flow statement always starts with the net income for the business listed at the top. The statement will then show a series of increases and decreases based on line items from the balance sheet. This is in an effort to modify the operating section of the cash flow statement and account for accrual accounting methods. Let me show you an example so it becomes clear. If a customer buys a $500 widget on credit, the sale has been made but the cash has not yet been received. The revenue is still recognized at the time of sale and in the month it was sold but the cash is now listed as an asset on the balance sheet. Let's say this company's net income is $10,000. The cash flow statement would start with this net income of $10,000 listed at the top. We know that this net income is overstated by $500 from the widget bought on credit. Here we would need a reduction of net income by the cash flow statement in the amount of the $500. This will bring the net income down to the true cash balance and this new total will be $9,500 which will be recorded under the cash flow from operations. Now I know that this all sounds like complicated accounting stuff but let me tell you once you get the handle of it, it will become very clear why it's important to understand it and you'd rather know it before you own a business than trying to figure it out afterwards. Now this is a very simple and straightforward example. The company you're probably buying might not be selling widgets and it might not take sales on credit but there will be other instances of net income not matching cash that you should be able to spot. The indirect method reconciles these discrepancies and gives you the true picture of the business health at a moment in time. Now that you can understand the importance of the cash flow statement as well as how it's typically structured and calculated we can move on to how to tactically use this information in the cash flow statement. The cash flow statement should be used as a planning tool. It provides a snapshot of the cash surplus or deficits your company may be facing which allows you to plan for growth or hunker down for harder times. Cash is variable over the year. Our job as a business owner is to understand where these low points are and to ensure that we have enough cash on hand to operate the business despite slow months. On the flip side an excess of cash is an indicator that the business is healthy and might be ready for growth and reinvestment. We will go into a little more detail in the financial due diligence section of other types of analysis you should be doing using the cash flow statement but that's for another module. And with that I think we can wrap up this lesson on the basic structure and usefulness of income statements, balance sheets and cash flow statements.
